paragraphs.text: Then Judah took a wife for himself and had by her three sons: Er, Onan, and Shelah. Er, his firstborn, took Tamar to wife. “And Er… was wicked before the Lord” — that is, behaved ungodly before God — “and the Lord slew him” (Gen 38:7). Er’s brother, Onan, loved Tamar and took her to wife. But since he hated his brother, he did not wish to raise up seed to him. And when God, for the wickedness contrived by Onan, “slew him also” (Gen 38:10), then it seemed to Judah that both of Tamar’s husbands had perished because of her sins, and he sent her to her father’s house in the hope that she would marry, “till Shelah be grown” (Gen 38:11). But when Shelah grew up, and Judah still did not bring Tamar back to his house, Tamar began to ponder within herself: “How can I make the Hebrews understand that I do not desire marriage, but the blessing hidden among them is dear to me? By refusing to marry Shelah, I can show my abstinence. But by entering into marriage with him, I cannot make my faith known. Would it not be better for me to have Judah himself as husband? Then the treasure I have gained will enrich my poverty, and the widowhood I keep will prove that marriage is not desirable to me.” And since she feared that Judah, recognizing her, might kill her in anger over his two sons, for whose death he blamed her, she — like Eliezer — asked for a sign for herself and said: “It is open to Thy all-knowing that in my act there is no fleshly desire. I am sure within myself that I desire only what is hidden in the Hebrews; but I do not know whether this is pleasing or not pleasing to Thee. Grant that I appear different to Judah, lest he kill me when his lips pronounce judgment upon me. This will sufficiently teach me that it is pleasing to Thee that the treasure hidden in the circumcised be given to men through a daughter of the uncircumcised. Therefore let Judah say to me: ‘I will come in unto thee’” (Gen 38:16).

When Tamar prayed thus, Judah enters and sees her, and contrary to his custom turns aside to her as to a harlot, because Tamar’s prayer drew him. But as soon as Tamar saw Judah, she covered her face out of fear. And when his lips pronounced the very word of the sign she had asked, she understood that her deed was pleasing to God. And then without fear she uncovers her face, even asks a reward from the one who possessed the treasure. Judah gives her his staff, his ring, and his bracelet; and Tamar received three witnesses, who bore witness to the Mediator who was by succession to come forth through her, and then returned to her father’s house.

“And it came to pass about three months after, that it was told Judah, saying: Tamar… hath played the harlot; and behold, she is with child by whoredom” (Gen 38:24). And Judah, seeing that it would be hard for her to justify herself, ordered her to be burned. The inhabitants of Hebron had already gathered to escort her to be burned. Then Tamar produces her witnesses and sends them to Judah with his kinsmen, asking that they say to him: “By the man whose these are, am I with child” (Gen 38:25). And when Judah saw his pledges, he marveled at the woman’s faith. Already stretching out his hand to take them, he turned his thought to the time when he had given them to Tamar, and said: “She hath been more righteous than I” (Gen 38:26) — that is, she is as much more righteous than I as my sons were more sinful, because I did not give her Shelah, my son. She is righteous, while I by a single evil thought of mine refused her my son Shelah. So Judah, who had unjustly refused Tamar when she asked for lawful marriage, justified her when she fell into harlotry. And her whom he had cast out for the death of his first two sons, he brought back to himself and received into his house in place of two other sons, but did not have her for his wife, because she was the wife of his first two sons. Nor did he take another wife, because she was the mother of his other two sons.
